title: AI-First Enterprise Operations: Reinventing the Operating System of Work
gist: BCG argues the value gap in enterprise AI comes not from better models but from redesigning entire processes for agentic execution: 60% of firms have yet to capture material value, copilots yield only 10-20% gains, while early agentic clients see 3x productivity, 80% cycle-time cuts and 60%+ cost reductions. It lays out a framework for agentic operations and argues platform lock-in is a fading concern as portability rises.

insights.claim: 60% of organizations have yet to capture material value at scale from AI, and early copilot/bot deployments delivered productivity gains of only 10% to 20%.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: Confirms most enterprise AI spend is still failing to scale—skepticism warranted on vendors touting copilots as transformation.
insights.quote: Many organizations have taken their first steps with AI—deploying copilots, bots, and an “automation layer”—yet 60% have yet to capture material value at scale.

insights.claim: BCG's first agentic clients are demonstrating a three-fold productivity increase, an 80% reduction in cycle time, and 60% or more in long-term cost reductions—far above copilot-era results.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: Quantifies the prize from full agentic redesign—a step-change that could reset cost structures in process-heavy industries.
insights.quote: our first agentic clients are demonstrating a three-fold increase in productivity, an 80% reduction in cycle time, and 60% or more in long-term cost reductions.

insights.claim: The driver of AI value is not superior models but end-to-end process redesign for agentic AI, with outcome-managed agents and agent-driven control flow and orchestration.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: Implies competitive edge accrues to operational redesign capability, not model access—favoring integrators over pure model providers.
insights.quote: The organizations generating real value with AI do not have superior AI models. Instead, they have redesigned processes end-to-end for agentic AI
insights.locator: Agentic Enterprise Operations Rewrite the AI Paradigm

insights.claim: A leading European bank using BCG's OpsAI Agent in retail lending achieved over 90% end-to-end automation for consumer loans, over 70% for mortgages, and productivity gains exceeding 50% across retail lending processes.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that agentic automation can gut back-office cost in lending—relevant to bank opex and fintech disruption theses.
insights.quote: end-to-end automation of more than 90% for consumer loans, more than 70% for mortgage loans, and, more broadly, productivity gains across retail lending processes exceeded 50%.
insights.locator: For example, a leading European bank

themes.theme: From optimizing tasks to governing outcomes

insights.claim: When agentic execution becomes near-instant and capacity is no longer the constraint, the core design question shifts from optimizing process flow to governing outcomes.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: A mental model for assessing which firms truly grasp the agentic shift versus those merely automating tasks.
insights.quote: When execution becomes nearly instant, and capacity is no longer a constraint, the design question shifts from “How do we optimize the flow?” to “How do we govern outcomes?”
insights.locator: From Optimizing Tasks to Designing Autonomy

insights.claim: Agentic operations enable new business models including outcome-based offerings and personalization at scale, as near-instant execution removes the human-capacity bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: Outcome-based pricing could reshape revenue models in services—watch for early movers monetizing agentic capacity.
insights.quote: Business models evolve. Near-instant execution and personalization at scale enable new value propositions, such as outcome-based offerings and tailored services.
insights.locator: From Optimizing Tasks to Designing Autonomy

insights.claim: BCG advises placing a single-platform bet now, arguing lock-in is not a major concern because portability is expected to increase and next-gen architectures will mix agents across platforms via shared protocols.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: Bearish on platform-lock-in moats for agentic vendors; bullish on interoperability/protocol layers as a battleground.
insights.quote: Pick one agentic platform and get started; lock-in is not a major concern as portability is expected to increase as agentic AI matures.

insights.claim: BCG expects no one-size-fits-all agent platform; most enterprises will land on a hybrid, multivendor setup and platforms will likely converge over time.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: Multivendor reality favors orchestration/integration players over any single agentic platform winner-take-all narrative.
insights.quote: There is currently no one-size-fits-all agent platform. Most enterprises will likely land on a hybrid, and platforms will likely converge.

insights.claim: BCG identifies the tech and data foundation—trusted data access, production-grade runtime/orchestration beyond the LLM, governed workflow integration, and AI-specific testing—as the common blocker to scaling multistep autonomy.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: The data/runtime stack, not the model, gates agentic ROI—supportive for data-platform and observability/orchestration vendors.
insights.quote: A common blocker to multistep autonomy at scale we see across our clients is the tech and data foundation.

insights.claim: BCG observes that context and data access are likely to become the scaling bottlenecks, so agentic platforms should sit close to the data platform.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: Reinforces that proximity to enterprise data is the strategic high ground in the agentic stack.
insights.quote: Context and access are likely to become scaling bottlenecks. As such, agentic platforms should sit close to the data platform.

insights.claim: BCG frames agentic transformation along two design choices—greenfield vs. brownfield, and full vs. partial shift to agentic AI—and suggests building a greenfield agentic 'attacker' to disrupt your own business and transfer learnings to legacy operations.
insights.speaker: Marcus Wittig
insights.so_what: A lens for judging incumbents' AI strategies—those launching internal greenfield attackers signal seriousness about self-disruption.
insights.quote: build a greenfield agentic attacker to disrupt your own business and learn how to transfer learnings to your brownfield operations.
